methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $1.0B - $23.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.6x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Cerebras
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $23B

private.name: Ouster
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1.5B

private.name: Aeva Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Credo Semiconductor is a publicly traded semiconductor company that went public via IPO in 2022 and currently carries a valuation above $5B. The valuation exercise draws on a universe of 3 public peers in the Chips & Semiconductors sector (Cerebras, Ouster, and Aeva Technologies) alongside 8 additional private frontier-tech comparables.

Peer-median valuation is the sole applicable triangulation method, given the absence of disclosed revenue for the target. Three same-sector, same-stage public peers yield a median valuation of $1.5B, with a range spanning $1.0B (Aeva Technologies) to $23.0B (Cerebras). Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples—median 20.6x across ALAB, NVDA, and AMD—cannot be applied without target revenue disclosure, a constraint typical of private companies and less common for public entities. The wide peer dispersion ($1.0B to $23.0B) reflects material heterogeneity in company scale and market positioning within the semiconductor frontier-tech space.

The peer-median method implies a valuation range of $1.3B to $1.7B, with a central estimate of $1.5B. This analysis rests on a narrow methodological foundation and a small peer set, both of which constrain precision. The target's current $5B+ valuation materially exceeds all three direct comparables, introducing meaningful uncertainty around whether peer metrics meaningfully anchor valuation for a company of demonstrably larger scale or capitalization.
